name: Robert Wilson
description: Oneida/Choctaw Native American political prisoner who in 1978 exposed a government plot to assassinate AIM leader Leonard Peltier inside Marion federal prison, then aligned with Peltier and AIM. In 1984 he, Peltier, and Albert Garza fasted 42 days at Marion to protest denial of Native religious rights and were placed in total solitary isolation for 15 months. He spent decades in McAlester State Prison in Oklahoma on prior convictions; he died in prison on August 14, 1998.

cases.charges: Robbery and habitual offender convictions; later years at USP Marion in federal custody on related charges

name: Albert Nuh Washington
description: New Afrikan Black Liberation Army prisoner of war, born 1941 in New York City. A Black Panther Party member, he was convicted along with Anthony 'Jalil' Bottom and Herman Bell in the 1971 killings of two NYPD officers (the BLA case that ran parallel to the Dhoruba bin Wahad case). The prosecutor admitted he could not say what role Washington played but asked the jury to convict him on his beliefs. He served nearly 30 years, much of it in solitary, before dying of liver cancer in custody on April 28, 2000.

cases.charges: Murder of two NYPD officers (1971 BLA case)

name: Johnny Imani Harris
description: Black political prisoner from Birmingham, Alabama. Arrested at 19 in 1970 on a string of robbery and rape charges he says were fabricated; pressured by court-appointed lawyers to plead guilty to avoid the death penalty, he received five consecutive life sentences at Atmore Prison. While inside he organized with Inmates For Action; after the 1974 Atmore uprising in which a guard was killed, Harris was framed for the killing and sentenced to death by an all-white jury despite exculpatory eyewitness testimony. His death sentence was overturned and he remains serving life at Holman Correctional Facility in Alabama.

cases.charges: Robbery, rape (1970); murder of prison guard George Dobbins (1974 Atmore uprising)
cases.convicted: Yes (plea on 1970 charges; jury conviction on 1974 charges, later overturned)
cases.sentence: Five consecutive life sentences plus death sentence (overturned)

name: Richard Mafundi Lake
description: New Afrikan political prisoner and self-described soldier in the struggle for Black national liberation, born and raised in Birmingham, Alabama under Bull Connor and Klan terror. Convicted under Alabama's habitual offender (three-strikes) law on what supporters called a frame-up rape charge and sentenced to life without parole. He spent decades in Alabama state prisons including Bessemer, where he wrote and organized on Pan-African and prisoner rights issues until his death in custody.

cases.charges: Rape (habitual offender enhancement)

name: Ramona Africa
description: Sole adult survivor of the May 13, 1985 Philadelphia police bombing of the MOVE compound on Osage Avenue, which killed eleven MOVE members including five children. Charged with 21 counts including riot and conspiracy, she served as her own attorney and was convicted in 1986 of riot and conspiracy and sentenced to 16 months to 7 years. She was repeatedly denied parole for refusing to disassociate from MOVE; she served the maximum 7 years before being released in 1992. She remains a leading MOVE spokesperson in Philadelphia.

cases.charges: Riot, conspiracy, assaulting police (May 13, 1985 MOVE bombing)

name: Edwin Cortes
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born March 27, 1955 in Chicago, one of fifteen children. A founder of the Union for Puerto Rican Students and the Pedro Albizu Campos Collective, he helped establish the Latino Cultural Center in Chicago. Captured June 29, 1983 with two compatriots and charged with seditious conspiracy as a member of the FALN (Armed Forces of National Liberation). Sentenced to 35 years and held at Lewisburg USP under severe conditions. Released from federal prison in 1999 after President Clinton commuted his sentence along with other FALN prisoners.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, weapons charges (FALN)

name: Elizam Escobar
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war, painter, and poet born May 24, 1948 in Ponce, Puerto Rico. Earned a BA in Visual Arts from the University of Puerto Rico and taught at the Museo del Barrio's School of the Arts in New York. Captured April 4, 1980 with other FALN combatants in Evanston, Illinois, and convicted of seditious conspiracy. Served 19 years of a 68-year sentence in federal prisons including Oxford and El Reno; his paintings created in prison were widely exhibited. Released in 1999 by President Clinton's commutation. Died in 2021.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy (FALN)

name: Oscar Lopez Rivera
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born January 6, 1943 in San Sebastian, Puerto Rico. Moved to Chicago at age 12, decorated Vietnam veteran, and founder of the Rafael Cancel Miranda High School and other independentista community institutions. Went underground in 1976 and was captured May 29, 1981 after being on the FBI's most wanted list; convicted of seditious conspiracy as a leader of the FALN. Sentenced to 55 years, with additional 15 years added for conspiracy to escape from Leavenworth. Refused Clinton's 1999 clemency offer because it excluded comrades; finally released January 17, 2017 after President Obama commuted his sentence. He served nearly 36 years, becoming the longest-held Puerto Rican political prisoner of his era.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery, conspiracy to escape (FALN)

name: Ida Luz Rodriguez
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born July 7, 1950 in Las Marias, Puerto Rico. Studied psychology and sociology at Northeastern Illinois University and was a leader at the Rafael Cancel Miranda Puerto Rican High School in Chicago. The companera of Oscar Lopez Rivera, she went underground in 1976 and was captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston, Illinois. After serving an 8-year state sentence she began an 80-year federal sentence for seditious conspiracy as a FALN member. Held under harsh conditions including the Davis Hall control unit at Alderson opened specifically for her and Haydee Torres. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Alejandrina Torres
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born June 18, 1939 in San Lorenzo, Puerto Rico. Founding teacher at the Rafael Cancel Miranda Puerto Rican High School in Chicago and active in independentista community organizing. Captured in 1983 and convicted of seditious conspiracy as a FALN member. She was one of the first three women held in the underground Lexington High Security Unit (with Susan Rosenberg and Silvia Baraldini), a women's control unit later closed by court order as illegal. She suffered a heart attack and physical assault in custody. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation. Died in 2018.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, weapons charges (FALN)

name: Ricardo Jimenez
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born April 3, 1956 in San Sebastian, Puerto Rico. Raised in Chicago, he was active in struggles at Tuley High School and studied at Loyola University and the Chicago Institute of Technology. Captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston, Illinois with other FALN combatants. Sentenced to 90 years for seditious conspiracy and related charges. Held at multiple federal prisons including Lewisburg and Stateville. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation after 19 years in custody.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Dylcia Pagan
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born October 15, 1946 in 'El Barrio' New York City. Brooklyn College graduate in Cinematography, founder of the Puerto Rican Students Union, NYC school teacher, and television producer for ABC, NBC, and CBS. Captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston, Illinois; sentenced to 55 years for seditious conspiracy as a FALN member, after serving an 8-year state sentence. Held in solitary at Cook County, Dwight, and Pleasanton. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Carlos Alberto Torres
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born September 19, 1952 in Ponce, Puerto Rico. Co-founder of the Rafael Cancel Miranda Puerto Rican High School in Chicago. Went underground in 1976 and was placed on the FBI's ten most wanted list; captured in 1980 and sentenced to 78 years for seditious conspiracy as a FALN member. Held at multiple federal prisons including Talladega. Released July 26, 2010 on parole after serving 30 years; was one of the last FALN POWs released, having refused Clinton's 1999 clemency due to its conditions.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, weapons charges (FALN)

name: Haydee Beltran Torres
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born June 7, 1955 in Arecibo, Puerto Rico. A founder of the Rafael Cancel Miranda Puerto Rican High School in Chicago. Went underground in 1976 and was captured April 4, 1980. The first FALN POW to receive a life sentence, she was convicted in state court for the 1977 Mobil Oil bombing in New York. Spent years in the Davis Hall control unit at Alderson, opened specifically for her and Ida Luz Rodriguez. Released around 2009 after about 29 years in custody.

cases.charges: Murder, conspiracy (1977 Mobil Oil bombing, NYC)

name: Alberto Rodriguez
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born April 14, 1953 in New York City and raised in Chicago. Earned a BS in Political Science from the University of Illinois at Chicago and was a co-founder of the Union of Puerto Rican Students. A community organizer in housing and education struggles, he was active in the National Committee to Stop the Grand Jury. Captured June 29, 1983 with Edwin Cortes and Alejandrina Torres; convicted of seditious conspiracy and sentenced to 35 years as a FALN member. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, weapons charges (FALN)

name: Adolfo Matos
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born September 18, 1950 in Lajas, Puerto Rico. Moved to New York City as a young man and later to Chicago; a skilled artist working in copper etchings of Puerto Rican historical figures. Captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston, Illinois with other FALN combatants. Sentenced to 70 years for seditious conspiracy. Held at multiple Illinois and federal prisons including Lompoc, California. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Luis Rosa
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born August 6, 1960 in Chicago. President of the Union of Puerto Rican Students at the University of Illinois Chicago, community activist on police brutality, poet, and conguero. Captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston at age 19. Sentenced to 30 years on state charges and 55 years federal for seditious conspiracy as a FALN member. Held in Joliet and other Illinois prisons. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Alicia Rodriguez
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born October 21, 1954 in Chicago, sister of Ida Luz Rodriguez. Studied biology at the University of Illinois at Chicago. Captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston, Illinois with her sister and other FALN compatriots. Sentenced to 30 years on state charges with 55 years federal awaiting on seditious conspiracy charges. Held primarily at Dwight Correctional Center in Illinois. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Carmen Valentin
description: Puerto Rican prisoner of war born March 2, 1946 in Arecibo, Puerto Rico. Master's in Spanish from Northeastern Illinois University; counselor at Tuley High School in Chicago where she organized against a 73 percent Puerto Rican dropout rate. Captured April 4, 1980 in Evanston with other FALN combatants. Sentenced to 90 years for seditious conspiracy. Held at Dwight, Alderson, and Pleasanton federal prisons. Released September 10, 1999 by President Clinton's commutation.

cases.charges: Seditious conspiracy, armed robbery (FALN)

name: Dora Garcia
description: Puerto Rican political prisoner born July 10, 1950 in Arecibo, Puerto Rico. Counselor at Cathedral Shelter in Chicago, member of the Chicago Coalition for the Homeless and the National Committee to Free Puerto Rican Prisoners of War. Convicted of conspiracy related to a 1988 attempt to assist in the escape of Oscar Lopez Rivera; served a three-year sentence at FCI Pleasanton, California.
affiliation: National Committee to Free Puerto Rican Prisoners of War

cases.charges: Conspiracy to assist escape

name: Jaime Delgado
description: Puerto Rican political prisoner born April 9, 1953 in Newark, New Jersey. Counselor at Proyecto Palante at Northeastern Illinois University, founding member of the Committee to Free the Five Nationalists, and former national coordinator of the National Committee to Free Puerto Rican Prisoners of War. Testified before the UN Decolonization Committee on Puerto Rico. Convicted of conspiracy related to the 1988 attempt to assist in the escape of Oscar Lopez Rivera; served a four-year sentence at USP Terre Haute.
affiliation: National Committee to Free Puerto Rican Prisoners of War

cases.charges: Conspiracy to assist escape

name: Filiberto Ojeda Rios
description: Puerto Rican independence leader born April 26, 1933 in Naguabo, Puerto Rico. Founder and Comandante of the clandestine armed group Los Macheteros (Ejercito Popular Boricua). Arrested in 1970 in Puerto Rico for allegedly organizing MIRA; charges dismissed in 1980. Arrested again August 30, 1985 in the FBI roundup of Macheteros suspects in the 1983 Wells Fargo armored car expropriation in West Hartford, Connecticut. Held in pretrial detention in New York and Connecticut; jumped bail in 1990 and lived clandestinely in Puerto Rico. Killed by FBI agents at his home in Hormigueros on September 23, 2005 in a controversial operation.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, armed robbery (1983 Wells Fargo Hartford expropriation)
cases.convicted: Convicted in absentia after jumping bail in 1990

name: Patricia Gros Levasseur
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner from Maryland's eastern shore. Wife of Raymond Luc Levasseur, she lived clandestinely with him for ten years as part of the United Freedom Front, which carried out bombings against U.S. corporate and military targets in solidarity with anti-apartheid and Central American struggles. Arrested in November 1984 and prosecuted in the seditious conspiracy and RICO trial of the Ohio 7 in Springfield, Massachusetts. Served her sentence and was released; the Ohio 7 sedition charges ended in mistrial and the prosecution dropped them in 1989.

cases.charges: Harboring fugitives, false identification (Ohio 7 / United Freedom Front)

name: Raymond Luc Levasseur
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner born October 10, 1946 in Sanford, Maine, of French-Canadian background. Vietnam veteran (1967) and SSOC organizer in Tennessee; served three years in Tennessee State Prison for a $7 marijuana sale. Organizer with VVAW and the prison-organizing group SCAR. Went underground in 1974 and became a leader of the Sam Melville/Jonathan Jackson Unit and the United Freedom Front, claiming responsibility for at least 16 bombings against U.S. military, corporate, and apartheid-linked targets. Captured November 4, 1984 in Ohio. Convicted of conspiracy and bombings and sentenced to 45 years; acquitted with codefendants of seditious conspiracy after a 10-month trial in 1989. Released from federal prison in 2004 after 20 years; spent much of that time at USP Marion.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, bombing of government and corporate buildings (United Freedom Front)

name: Barbara Curzi-Laaman
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner born November 10, 1957 in Boston, of Italian immigrant background. Wife of Jaan Laaman, lived clandestinely with him and their three children for two and a half years as part of the United Freedom Front. Arrested November 4, 1984. Received a 15-year sentence for conspiracy and bombings carried out against U.S. support for South African apartheid and the Central American wars. Tried again in 1989 for seditious conspiracy, which ended in mistrial. Released after serving her federal sentence.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, bombing (United Freedom Front)

name: Jaan Karl Laaman
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner born March 21, 1948, Estonian-born and raised in Boston and Buffalo. SDS activist in the 1960s. Imprisoned 1972-1978 for bombing Nixon's reelection headquarters and a police station in Manchester, New Hampshire. Went underground in the 1980s as a member of the United Freedom Front and the Sam Melville/Jonathan Jackson Unit. Captured November 1984 with wife Barbara Curzi-Laaman. Convicted of multiple bombings and sentenced to 98 years federal; acquitted with codefendants of seditious conspiracy in 1989. Released from federal prison in June 2021 after about 37 years in custody.

cases.charges: Bombings, conspiracy (United Freedom Front, Sam Melville/Jonathan Jackson Unit)

name: Carol Manning
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner born January 3, 1956 in Kezar Falls, Maine, daughter of woolen mill workers. Active in community and prison organizing through the SCAR collective in Portland, Maine. Wife of Tom Manning, lived underground from 1976 until her April 1985 arrest. Convicted of bombings carried out in support of struggles in South Africa and Central America and sentenced to 15 years. Indicted in May 1986 with seven others for seditious conspiracy and RICO; sedition charges ended in mistrial 1989 and were dropped.

cases.charges: Bombings, conspiracy (United Freedom Front)

name: Thomas William Manning
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner born June 28, 1946 in Boston to an Irish working-class family. Vietnam veteran (1965-66); served five years in Massachusetts state prison for armed robbery in the late 1960s, where he became politicized in Walpole's 10 Block. Co-founder of SCAR in Maine. Went underground for ten years as part of the Sam Melville/Jonathan Jackson Unit and the United Freedom Front. Captured in 1985 and convicted of the self-defense killing of New Jersey State Trooper Philip Lamonaco; sentenced to life in New Jersey state prison and 53 years federal for the UFF bombings. Died in federal custody at FMC Butner on July 30, 2019 after nearly 35 years in prison.

cases.charges: Murder of NJ State Trooper Philip Lamonaco (1981); federal bombings (UFF)

name: Richard Charles Williams
description: Ohio 7 political prisoner born November 4, 1947 in Beverly, Massachusetts. Active with the Prairie Fire Organizing Committee and the New England Free Press; provided security at the 1979 Amandla anti-apartheid concert and went to Greensboro to protest the Klan/Nazi murders of CWP members. Went underground in 1981 and was captured in Cleveland in 1984. Convicted of bombings as part of the United Freedom Front and the killing of NJ State Trooper Philip Lamonaco. Died in federal custody at USP Marion on December 6, 2005 of liver failure.

cases.charges: Bombings, conspiracy, murder of NJ State Trooper Lamonaco
cases.sentence: 45 years federal plus 35 years to life (NJ)

name: Alan Berkman
description: North American anti-imperialist political prisoner born September 4, 1945 in Middletown, New York. Columbia-trained physician who provided medical care to oppressed communities in New York, Alabama, and Boston, and during the 1973 occupation of Wounded Knee. Jailed nine months in 1982 as a grand jury resister. Indicted for clandestinely treating a wounded BLA fugitive after Nyack, went underground, and was captured by the FBI in Philadelphia in May 1985. Held two years in preventive detention while undiagnosed lymphoma worsened. Convicted in the Resistance Conspiracy case (bombings of the U.S. Capitol, Navy Yard, FBI, and military targets) and sentenced to 12 years. Released in 1992; died of cancer June 5, 2009.

cases.charges: Conspiracy, possession of weapons and explosives, bombing of U.S. Capitol and military installations (Resistance Conspiracy case)

name: Timothy Blunk
description: North American anti-imperialist political prisoner born May 21, 1957 in New Jersey. Hampshire College graduate, student organizer in Western Massachusetts. Served nine months at Rikers Island for the 1981 anti-apartheid action against the South African Springboks rugby tour. Arrested with Susan Rosenberg November 29, 1984 in Cherry Hill, NJ in possession of weapons, explosives, and false identification; both took the political position of captured combatants and received unprecedented 58-year sentences. Indicted in 1988 in the Resistance Conspiracy case. Released from federal custody in March 2003 after about 19 years.

cases.charges: Possession of weapons, explosives, false identification; Resistance Conspiracy bombings

name: Larry W. Giddings
description: North American anti-imperialist political prisoner born October 6, 1952 in Bavaria, West Germany. Wounded and arrested August 21, 1971 in Los Angeles for an arms expropriation; sentenced to 20-to-life. Released 1978 under new sentencing law. Wounded and re-arrested October 14, 1979 in Seattle during an attempted armed liberation of a comrade from jail (Bill Dunne case); convicted of escape aiding, bank expropriations, shooting a policeman, and conspiracy. Received multiple consecutive life and 75-year sentences. Held primarily at Leavenworth from 1982. Released from federal prison in 2019.

cases.charges: Bank expropriation, escape aiding, shooting policeman, conspiracy

name: Edward Allen Mead
description: North American political prisoner and member of the George Jackson Brigade, which carried out approximately 20 bombings in the Pacific Northwest from 1975-1978 in support of prisoner struggles, the Pine Ridge resistance, and against the FBI and BIA. Arrested January 23, 1976 in Tukwila, Washington during a botched bank expropriation in which comrade Bruce Sidel was shot dead by police. Sentenced to two consecutive life terms (state) for first-degree assault on police plus 25 years federal for the bank robbery. At Walla Walla he founded the prisoner organization Men Against Sexism and published the Marion Times and Red Dragon newsletters. Released in 1993; lives in Washington state.

cases.charges: First-degree assault on police (two counts), bank robbery
cases.sentence: Two consecutive life terms (state) plus 25 years (federal)

name: Joseph Patrick Doherty
description: Provisional IRA volunteer born January 20, 1955 in the New Lodge area of Belfast. Interned without charge at age 17 in 1972 under British internment. Arrested in 1980 with his IRA Active Service Unit after an ambush in which British Captain Herbert Westmacott was killed; escaped from Crumlin Road Jail during his 1981 trial. Came to the U.S. and was arrested by federal officials in New York on June 18, 1983 on immigration charges. Held at MCC New York for over five years while the U.S. fought to extradite him; in 1984 a federal judge ruled the IRA ambush was a political offense barring extradition. Spent over nine years in U.S. federal custody (mostly MCC and Otisville) before being deported back to Northern Ireland in February 1992. Released under the Good Friday Agreement in 1998.

cases.charges: Illegal entry; subject of British extradition request for IRA ambush of British Army patrol (Westmacott killing)
cases.convicted: No (held in extradition/deportation custody in U.S.)
cases.sentence: Held over 8.5 years pretrial / pending extradition in U.S.; deported to N. Ireland

name: George Michael Ostensen
description: Plowshares anti-nuclear political prisoner born December 22, 1954 in Freeport, New York. Lived in community at Jonah House in Baltimore. On August 16, 1987, he entered Project ELF, the U.S. Navy's first-strike trigger for nuclear submarines in northern Wisconsin, poured his blood, cut three transmission poles, and notched the center pole, shutting down the system for 29 hours. Convicted under Wisconsin's sabotage statute and sentenced to 33 months; parole was denied for not having served sufficient time for punishment.

cases.charges: Sabotage (Wisconsin statute 946.02(1)(a)) for ELF Plowshares action
